So basically the main girl (Angela) had her mom die due to an earthquake and she never got to meet her because she died while being pregnant, so the main girl Angela had to be saved by a C-section. Anyways this is important because her and the other girl (Katherine) go to the woods with a pentagram to try and “talk” to the dead mom (really stupid cause like it’s a pentagram) but anyways instead of summoning the dead mom they clearly summoned some demon that possess’s them, and yah basically the whole movie is about trying to save the 2 girls and in the end the demon says “choose which girl lives and which on dies” and out of selfishness and stress, Katherine’s father yells “I choose you Katherine!” Without even knowing that he killed his daughter because by choosing her he didn’t save her life but instead gave her soul to hell. So in the scene it shows her being dragged to hell while everyone else just sees her dead on the floor, and then because of this the other girl, Angela, is saved and yea that’s basically how the movie ends
@ufweffortNicely said! Katherine’s death was so emotional

True horror buff here, went to see it the first day. Is it as ¨good¨ as the original? Um,,,no. However, you really can´t/shouldn´t try to view it through that lens. Watching it and comparing it to the first film isn´t fair, or reasonable, and will leave you disappointed. The original coming out in 1973 was groundbreaking, there had never been anything like it seen before. The dialog, the special effects then, the profanity, the story of demonic possession, it was just shocking and unheard off. People were caught completely off guard, so the impact was staggering (fainting, heart attacks, women going into labor in theaters) What´s going to match something like that today (?) Nothing. It´s 2023. ¨Chucky¨, ¨Annabelle¨, ¨The Conjuring¨ (and so on) people have now ¨seen it all¨. The over the top gore of films like Saw, Terrifier, have tainted us, that, along with few original ideas, there´s no shock value left. Keeping expectations in prospective, and accepting it for what it was, although somewhat disjointed in places, I enjoyed it, and I believe most fans of the original will also. There´s also a nice surprise at the end to set up the second chapter of the trilogy. Go see it, you know you want to, ¨THE POWER OF CHRIST COMPELS YOU!¨

I was 5 yrs old, when my older cousins took me to see the original, at the Cleveland Park  theaters (now called Cineplex-something or other) in Wash/DC, my hometown. Cleveland Park, is a very old, upper-NorthWest neighborhood in DC..and its walking distance to Georgetown Univ. Lets just say it has an "affect" to it, that lends itself to the creepiness of the house that the Exorcist was filmed in, on the campus of the school. After seeing "The Exorcist", I couldnt sleep in my room for almost 2 weeks. I slept with my folks. But I also became a horror movie fan because I saw The Exorcist. This "flanker" is a VERY far-cry from the original that literally changed how horror flicks were made. Friedkin, is a "horror god" because of the original.

It just didn't work and we end up feeling sad and let down rather than amazed and triumphant.

Okay why the heck does Hollywood/Black Rock always have to freaking shoehorn political BS into stuff nowadays? I've seen the movie it was okay so far until the point where we get to the woman from the original movie talking to one of the fathers and she out right said the reason why she wasn't in the same room with the two priests in the original movie because "she wasn't part of their patriarchy". Wtf does that have to do with sexism? Was this movie made by people who never watched the original movie or hated it?! The two priests from the original movie wanted her out of the room just to keep her life and sanity safe! The two freaking priests ended up sacrificing their lives trying to save her daughter; if she was in the same room with them, she would've died as well or her daughter would die without the priests' help! And this is what she repays those heros?! By calling them "being sexist"?! Wtf?! The people who made this movie literally wrote her to be ungrateful!

After that, movie started going downwards as it continues to give religion in general the middle finger by killing off the random professional Exorcist, who wanted to help, but spared the idiots within the movie who are not professional Exorcists nor Exorcists in general; doing something like that without training is extremely dangerous! And the way they sacrifice one daughter for the other was so stupid; if they wanted both the girls safe, one of the adults should've sacrifice their own life to save both of them; but we didn't get that. It was a disappointment.

Apparently they completely recut the entire movie due to abysmal reactions from test screenings.

Apparently the version shown in July has a completely different plot that focused on three Demonic artifacts, one that the two girls accidentally find and that leads to their possession, Reagan is apparently trying to find these artifacts to get them to the church to be contained, they explicitly explain that this is a Demon called "Lamashtu" it's not "Pazuzu", and the Nurse character was a lot more shady and it was implied that she was either in league with the Demons, or secretly worshipped them, and she might have been the one who influenced the girls to do the séance in order to get them possessed.

The film was so poorly received that they edited it all down and that's why the film is all over the place and a complete mess.

Too young to watch the original when it came out but did read the book, The Exorist, at the curious age of 11. And was utterly riveted by the read. I eventually watched the film some years later and found its low-key presentation to be an utterly spooky outing. The BBC this week, celebrating its 50th anniversary, aired The Exorcist. The film remains timeless and as creepy as it was back then. (A family member texted: "watched it at 1am and still gives me goosebumps"). For me, no horror film since comes close. This latest attempt of a reboot  I suspect will remain without impact. Subtlety in the spooky storytelling in modern day dramas is lost, as a defeaning soundtrack, a chorus of "who-can-scream-the-loudest" and a predictable flashing lightbulb etc. as if begging the audience to jump out of their seat, takes its place. All resulting in a constant reminder that this is just a work of...fiction, And tonight's sleep will be sound.  Simplicity is sadly lost on modern-day horror writers and so, for me, I know this film will come, go and remain unremembered. And to all real-life exorcists out there:  it will be just another OTT horror flick.

I watched 'The Exorcist ' for the first time time in 1974 at the Odeon cinema in London's Leicester Square, and afterwards I was terrified at the thought of having to travel across London late at night to reach my hotel. I have never seen a more scary movie than this. I was only 19 at the time. Even now, in 2023, it's not shown on British tv without serious censorship, 50 years later. This new sequel opens at my local cinema today - I'm not so readily shocked these days ! It will be interesting though to see how it fares in comparison to the original.

Ok. I'm writing this review the morning after going to watch 'Exorcist Believer'. Like I said in my first comment, you need to have seen the original film when it was first released, and witnessed its impact on the audience, in order to make a fair comparison. Of course in 1974 there was no social media, no internet at all, and I doubt it was even promoted in TV advertising. I remember a dark poster, showing the priest in a dimly lit street, gazing up a long flight of steps. The haunting music of Tubular Bells was new to most as well. The effects and images used in the movie were at the time original and some deemed offensive enough to have the film banned for many years by media outlets of the day.
Times have changed. Like the original, 'Exorcist Believer' is a bit of a slow burner. It has its moments, as discussed by others, and a nice twist at the end, but over the years we have become immune to the 'shock' elements in movies of this nature. But I don't imagine that it can have the same impact that Linda Blair and co did in the Seventies, or still be talked about many years from now.
